metadata.title: Why Silencing a Repair Gene Is Better for the Patient Than Expressing It
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=#1F6F5C = MGMT methylated / repair silenced / drug works / good patient outcome; CRIMSON=#BF3339 = MGMT expressed / damage repaired / drug neutralized / bad patient outcome
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no full DNA methylation mechanism (DNMT1, maintenance methylation), no histone code, no IDH mutation connection, no azacitidine/decitabine mechanism, no other epigenetic biomarkers. One mechanism only: MGMT promoter methylation silences repair, allowing temozolomide damage to accumulate and kill tumor cells.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.name: Title
beats.narration_text: Two patients. Same glioblastoma, same surgery, same radiation, same chemotherapy. One responds. The other doesn't. The difference is a single methyl group.

beats.name: TwoPatients
beats.narration_text: Glioblastoma — the most aggressive brain tumor — kills most patients within 15 months. But some respond to temozolomide chemotherapy far better than others.

props.headline: Glioblastoma — the most aggressive brain tumor — kills most patients…

beats.scene_description: Two medical charts side by side, pinned to newsprint — same diagnosis header, same treatment protocol listed — patient A and patient B, editorial collage style, desaturated

beats.name: SameTreatment
beats.narration_text: Same drug: temozolomide. It works by attaching a methyl group to the O6 position of guanine in tumor DNA — a lesion that jams replication and kills the cell.

beats.name: TheQuestion
beats.narration_text: DNA repair should protect a cell. A tumor that fixes its DNA more efficiently should survive better — and yet the patient whose tumor repairs DNA efficiently does worse. Why?

beats.name: NaiveExpectation
beats.narration_text: The naive expectation: more DNA repair is better. Cancer treatment damages tumor DNA; we want that damage to kill the cancer. A cell that fixes damage should die less.

beats.name: WhyItFlips
beats.narration_text: But here is the inversion: the drug is trying to damage the tumor. MGMT's only job is to reverse exactly that damage. When MGMT works, it neutralizes the drug — in minutes.

beats.name: DecisionTree
beats.narration_text: MGMT status splits patients into two paths. Methylated promoter: no MGMT protein made, O6-methylguanine lesions accumulate, tumor cells die. Unmethylated: MGMT expressed, damage repaired, drug wasted.

beats.name: SuicideMechanism
beats.narration_text: MGMT is a suicide enzyme. It transfers the methyl group from the O6-guanine onto its own cysteine — one repair per molecule, then the protein destroys itself. It cannot be recycled.

beats.name: MethylSilences
beats.narration_text: In the methylated patient, cytosines in the MGMT promoter carry methyl marks. Transcription machinery cannot access the gene. No MGMT mRNA. No protein. The repair capacity is permanently off.

beats.name: Biomarker
beats.narration_text: MGMT methylation is now standard in glioblastoma management: a predictive biomarker. Not prognostic — it does not predict survival regardless of treatment. It predicts benefit from this specific drug.

beats.name: ExampleTrace
beats.narration_text: Follow one lesion in each patient (illustrative). Patient A: O6-methylguanine forms, MGMT absent, lesion persists through replication, mismatch-repair triggers apoptosis — tumor cell dies. Patient B: lesion forms, MGMT transfers the methyl group in roughly 2 minutes — cell survives, drug wasted.

beats.name: Endcard
beats.narration_text: DNA repair should protect the cell — but when the drug is trying to destroy that cell, repair protects the wrong side. An epigenetic mark that silences the repair gene becomes, for the patient, a stroke of luck.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Biology series.
